personas.name: Application Developer
personas.role: Embedding haematite as a storage engine

stories.text: As an application developer, I want to create a haematite database with a single function call so that I do not need to manage shard actors, WAL files, or tree structures manually.
As an application developer, I want to put and get key-value pairs without thinking about which shard owns them so that the sharding is transparent.
As an application developer, I want range queries to return results in sorted key order so that I can scan sequential data efficiently.
As an application developer, I want to batch multiple writes into a single atomic commit so that my data is always in a consistent state.
As an application developer, I want the database to recover automatically from crashes so that I do not need to write recovery logic.

personas.name: Aion Engine
personas.role: Using haematite as the EventStore backend

stories.text: As the Aion engine, I want to append events to a workflow key with optimistic concurrency (expected_seq) so that two concurrent appends to the same workflow are detected and one is rejected.
As the Aion engine, I want to read a workflow's full event history in sequence order so that I can replay it for recovery.
As the Aion engine, I want to read events from a specific sequence number forward so that I can resume replay without re-reading the entire history.
As the Aion engine, I want to batch-append WorkflowStarted and SearchAttributesUpdated atomically so that a crash between them never leaves an orphaned workflow.

personas.name: Concurrent Writer
personas.role: Writing to haematite from multiple beamr processes simultaneously

stories.text: As a concurrent writer, I want to write to different keys from hundreds of beamr processes simultaneously without any of them blocking so that write throughput scales linearly with shard count.
As a concurrent writer, I want a crashed shard to restart without affecting my writes to other shards so that fault isolation is per-shard, not per-database.
As a debugging developer, I want to get the current root hash of a committed database state so that I can identify and compare snapshots.
As a debugging developer, I want to diff two database states by root hash so that I can see exactly which keys changed between them.
As a debugging developer, I want the diff to run in time proportional to the number of differences, not the total database size, so that diffing large databases with small changes is fast.
